coredata.dc:title: Sustainable and highly-stable carbon dots from cellulose as a fluorescent reference for visual ratiometric pH detection
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Kunfeng
Ji, Xin
Zhang, Jinming
Zhang, Xiaocheng
Nan, Qinying
Zhang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent materials, including organic molecules and quantum dots, usually respond to the environmental changes, such as acidity and basicity. It is challenging to construct highly-stable fluorescent materials. In this work, we fabricated novel carbon dots (CDs) with a high stability and a high quantum yield by using a solvothermal process, in which cellulose was the green precursor and 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl)/N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was the solvent. The resultant CDs had the cationic imidazolium groups on their surface. As a result, they exhibited the excellent fluorescence stability in strong-acidic, strong-basic and high salt solutions. In addition, the CDs also presented a good resistance to the photobleaching under strong UV irradiation conditions. The highly stable CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent reference to significantly improve the responsiveness of the fluorescent materials. For example, the CDs were combined with cellulose acetate fluorescein isothiocyanate (CA-FITC) to obtain a series of ratiometric probes, which can visually detect pH values with a higher sensitivity. Furthermore, based on the outstanding processability and formability of cellulose derivatives, the CDs/CA-FITC was easily fabricated into responsive fluorescent coatings, films, and inks, indicating a huge potential in visual detection, solid-state luminescence, and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Carbon dots
High stability
Fluorescent probe
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of cellulose acetate fluorescein isothiocyanate (CA-FITC)
Preparation and characterization of CDs from cellulose

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U2004211 and 52173292 ), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (No. 2018040 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic route for highly stable CDs; (b) TEM image and particle size distribution of CDs; (c) N 1s high-resolution spectra of CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission intensity of CDs in different pH buffer solutions; (b) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of various metal ions; (c) Fluorescence intensity of CDs upon addition of different concentrations of NaCl; (d) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of various anions; (e) Fluorescence intensity of CDs upon addition of different concentrations of Na2SO4; (f) Optical stability of CDs irradiated by a 365 nm lamp with a power of 10 W. (The concentration of CDs was 1.25 mg/mL)

(a) Schematic diagram of the ratiometric fluorescent probes from CDs and CA-FITC for pH detection; (b) Visible and fluorescence photographs, (c) fluorescence spectra, (d) fluorescence intensity and I green /I blue (e) CIE coordinate of CDs/CA-FITC ratiometric fluorescent probe. (The concentrations of CDs and CA-FITC were 1.25 mg/mL and 4 mg/mL, respectively.)

(a) CDs/CA-FITC smart labels for pH detection; (b) CDs/CA, CDs/CA-FITC and CA-FITC films with different fluorescence colors; (c) Reversible pH-responsive behavior of CDs/CA-FITC after adding NH3·H2O and HCl·H2O; (d) Monitoring of shrimp freshness by the smart label.

head.ce:title: Sustainable and highly-stable carbon dots from cellulose as a fluorescent reference for visual ratiometric pH detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhongguancun North First Street 2,100190 Beijing, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent materials, including organic molecules and quantum dots, usually respond to the environmental changes, such as acidity and basicity. It is challenging to construct highly-stable fluorescent materials. In this work, we fabricated novel carbon dots (CDs) with a high stability and a high quantum yield by using a solvothermal process, in which cellulose was the green precursor and 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl)/N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was the solvent. The resultant CDs had the cationic imidazolium groups on their surface. As a result, they exhibited the excellent fluorescence stability in strong-acidic, strong-basic and high salt solutions. In addition, the CDs also presented a good resistance to the photobleaching under strong UV irradiation conditions. The highly stable CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent reference to significantly improve the responsiveness of the fluorescent materials. For example, the CDs were combined with cellulose acetate fluorescein isothiocyanate (CA-FITC) to obtain a series of ratiometric probes, which can visually detect pH values with a higher sensitivity. Furthermore, based on the outstanding processability and formability of cellulose derivatives, the CDs/CA-FITC was easily fabricated into responsive fluorescent coatings, films, and inks, indicating a huge potential in visual detection, solid-state luminescence, and anti-counterfeiting.

We used natural cellulose to fabricate novel carbon dots (CDs) which have an outstanding resistance to the strong-acidic condition, strong-basic condition, high-salty solution and photobleaching.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have wide applications in bioimaging [1], sensing [2], optoelectronic devices [3], energy conversion [4], and anti-counterfeiting [5], based on their advantages of good biocompatibility, low toxicity, adjustable luminescence performance, high fluorescence quantum yield, low cost and facile preparation process [6,7]. CDs are generally synthesized via using organic molecules as carbon source with a “bottom-up” route, such as hydrothermal [8,9], solvothermal [10,11] and microwave [12,13] methods. The surface of the prepared CDs is rich in organic functional groups, thus CDs exhibit responsive properties [14–18], excitation wavelength dependence [19] and high quantum yields [20]. For instance, the N-doped CDs with N-alkyl groups have evident pH-responsive behaviors [21]. The CDs with oxygen-containing groups on their surface, such as carboxyl, aldehyde and hydroxyl groups, display a fluorescence quenching upon meeting with certain ions, acids and bases [22,23]. Therefore, it is still challenging to construct highly-stable CDs, whose fluorescence properties do not change as the environment changes.

“Green” CDs are defined as the CDs synthesized by using the renewable natural resources as the carbon source, such as polysaccharides, lignin and proteins. Various green precursors have been attempted in order to obtain high performance CDs [24–28]. Cellulose is the most abundant natural polymer in the nature, and has many attractive advantages, such as easy availability, renewability, biodegradability, and biocompatibility. It is considered as one of the sustainable raw materials to realize the eco-friendly development of human society [29,30]. However, due to the strong hydrogen bonding network interactions, cellulose is extremely difficult to be dissolved and processed [31–33]. As a result, when cellulose is used as the precursor of CDs, the resultant CDs generally have low photoluminescence quantum yields and poor stability (Table S1).

In this work, we fabricated highly-stable CDs with a high quantum yield by a solvothermal method, in which cellulose was used as the precursor and 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl)/N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) as the solvent. The heteroatom N on the surface of CDs existed in the form of the imidazolium cation, thus leading to an excellent stability. The prepared CDs were used as a fluorescent reference to construct ratiometric fluorescent probes with improved responsiveness.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) with degree of polymerization (DP) of 220 was acquired from Beijing Fengli Jingqiu Commerce and Trade Company. 1-Allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) was provided by Shandong Zhongke Henglian Bio-based Materials Co., Ltd. Cellulose acetate (CA, DS = 2.45, Mn = 30,000) was supplied by Sichuan Push Acetati Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O) and barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O, 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9%), manganese (II) chloride (MnCl2, 99%), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), fluorescein isothiocyanate isomer I (FITC, 90%), dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL, 95%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH) and boric acid (H3BO3, 99.5%) were purchased from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Copric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, 99%) and cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 99%) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrogen chloride (HCl, 36–38%) and acetic acid (HAc, 99.5%) were purchased from Beijing Beihua Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd.

MCC (0.01–0.65 g) was dissolved in 9.0 g AmimCl under continuous mechanical stirring at 80 °C for 2 h. Then, DMF of 3.9 g was added dropwise into MCC/AmimCl solutions with the MCC contents of 0.1–5 wt%. The resultant MCC/AmimCl/DMF solutions were transferred into poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclaves (25 mL). Subsequently, the autoclaves were kept at specific temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C) for a period of time (6 h, 12 h, 18 h, 24 h, 36 h and 48 h). The autoclaves were cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained suspension solutions were purified through dialysis in a dialysis bag with a MWCO of 500 Da. Finally, the purified solutions were freeze-dried to obtain CDs powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cellulose acetate fluorescein isothiocyanate (CA-FITC)
ce:section.ce:para: CA of 6.3576 g (24 mmol) was dissolved in dry DMF of 120 mL. Similarly, FITC of 0.4673 g (1.2 mmol) was dissolved in dry DMF of 5 mL. Subsequently, the FITC/DMF solution was added dropwise into the CA/DMF solution, and DBTDL of 3 mL was added as the catalyst. The solution was stirred vigorously at 100 °C for 6 h. After reaction, the final product was precipitated in EtOH, washed three times, and dried under vacuum at 80 °C.

Aqueous solutions of metal ions (K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, and Fe3+) at a concentration of 10 mM were prepared in volumetric flasks. Then, the solutions were diluted to 1 mM. The CDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 2.5 mg/mL was mixed with the metal ions solution (1 mM) at a volume ratio of 1:1. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded.

Britton-Robinson (B–R) buffer solutions were prepared by using H3PO4, H3BO3, CH3COOH and NaOH [34]. The accurate pH value was measured by a pH meter. CDs aqueous solution (0.1 mL, 25 mg/mL) was added dropwise into B–R buffer solutions (1.9 mL, pH = 1.89, 3.01, 3.98, 4.94, 5.97, 7.01, 7.96, 8.96, 9.95, 10.97, 11.82). The zeta potential and fluorescence spectra of the solutions were recorded after mixing.

CA-FITC/DMF solutions (1.4 mL) with different concentrations were mixed with CDs/DMF solution (0.2 mL, 12.5 mg/mL). Then, B–R buffer solutions (0.3 mL) were added dropwise to the CDs/CA-FITC/DMF solutions. After mixing, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The filter paper was treated by DMSO to completely remove the luminogens. As a result, nonfluorescent filter paper was obtained. Then, the CDs/CA-FITC/DMF solution was used as the inks to print the patterns on the nonfluorescent filter paper. After drying in an oven at 80 °C for 2 h, the smart labels were obtained.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on a Hitachi HT-7700 instrument with an accelerating voltage of 120 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded in the angular range of 4–80° (2θ) with a scanning speed of 5°/min by using an X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands). The wavelength of the Cu Kα radiation source was 1.54 Å. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the ESCALab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) using 200 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet 6700 (Thermal Fisher) spectrometer by using KBr method. 1H NMR spectrum was recorded on a Bruker Avance 400 NMR spectrometer in DMSO‑d 6 and TMS as the internal standard. Zeta potential was recorded on a Malvern Zetasize Nano-ZS90 instrument. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was obtained using a Shimadzu UV 2600 spectrophotometer (ISR-2600Plus with an integrated sphere). Fluorescence spectrum was recorded with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Time-resolved decay spectrum was obtained on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) was obtained on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere. The accurate pH values were measured by a pH meter (Thermo Scientific).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs from cellulose
ce:section.ce:para: Biopolymer cellulose is one of the green precursors of CDs (Table S1). We used microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) as the carbon source, and AmimCl/DMF as the solvent. Via a solvothermal method, highly-stable CDs were obtained (Fig. 1 a). The effect of reaction conditions on the fluorescence properties of the as-prepared CDs were investigated (Fig. S1-S3). As the reaction temperature increases, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs firstly increases and then decreases, and the emission wavelength is redshifted. The temperature of 180 °C is the optimal temperature for the formation of CDs. The low temperature, such as 160 °C, is not conducive to the carbonization process of cellulose, thus less luminescent groups forms. The high temperature, such as 200 °C, leads to an excessive carbonization, thus partial luminescent groups are depleted. At 180 °C, when the content of MCC is 1.5%, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs is relatively high (Fig. S2). This is because the sufficient cellulose provides not only the carbon source but also the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which acts as fluorophores in CDs. The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) increases as the cellulose concentration firstly increases and then decreases (Fig. S4). Therefore, when the solvent is AmimCl/DMF, the cellulose concentration is 1.5%, and the reaction temperature is 180 °C, the synthesized CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence emission intensity and photoluminescence QY of 23%, which is better than the previous CDs prepared from cellulose (Table S1).

TEM image shows that CDs are uniform 0D nanoparticles with the average diameter of 5.57 nm (Fig. 1b). X-ray diffraction (XRD) curve (Fig. S5) demonstrates that the CDs are amorphous. The as-prepared CDs have only one diffraction peak at 2θ = 8.5°, indicating a layer spacing of 1.03 nm, which is much larger than the lattice spacing of graphite (0.34 nm). XPS survey spectrum exhibits that the CDs consist of carbon (80.82%), oxygen (11.01%), nitrogen (7.67%), and a small amount of chlorine (0.51%) (Fig. S6a), indicating that AmimCl participates in the formation of CDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.72 eV, 285.55 eV and 288.06 eV (Fig. S6b), corresponding to the C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO bonds, respectively. Similarly, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.92 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.71 eV (Fig. 1c), which are assigned to C–NC, graphite N and imidazolium N, respectively [35,36]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s is decomposed into two peaks at 531.2 eV (CO) and 532.3 eV (C–OH/C–O–C) (Fig. S6c). In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1d), the peaks at 3411 cm−1, 1646 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 1165 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1 are assigned to O–H, CO, C–N, C–O and imidazolium cation, respectively. These results demonstrate that the surface of CDs is rich in organic functional groups, including O–H, CO/CN, C–O, C–N and imidazolium cation. Moreover, these results confirm that the as-prepared CDs are nitrogen-doped CDs with imidazolium structure on their surface.

The CDs exhibit blue fluorescence. In the excitation wavelength range of 240–360 nm, the fluorescence of CDs is immune to the excitation wavelength, while in the excitation wavelength range of 360–450 nm, the fluorescence of CDs has an obvious excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. S7a). These phenomena indicate that the CDs have multiple photoluminescence centers. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum shows that CDs have a strong absorption peak at 208 nm and a broad absorption band at 250–400 nm (Fig. S7b). In the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs, there are two excitation bands with the centers at 265 nm and 340 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC/CN and the n-π* transition of CO/CN, respectively (Fig. S7c) [37]. Time-resolved decay spectrum of CDs demonstrates that the average lifetime of CDs fluorescence is 23.20 ns (Fig. S8 and Table S2).

The as-prepared nitrogen-doped CDs have an excellent optical stability. In a wide pH range of 1.89–11.82, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs is substantially unchanged (Fig. 2a and S9). That is to say, the CDs can maintain a stable fluorescence emission in either strong acidic solution or strong basic solution. The high optical stability of CDs in a wide pH range is because the heteroatom N on the surface of CDs mainly exists in the form of imidazolium nitrogen, which is in a saturated coordination state and does not undergo movement of electrons. Thus, the fluorescence emission of CDs is free from H+ and OH−. The Zeta potential of CDs is always positive in a pH range of 1.89–11.82 (Fig. S10), confirming the above mechanism further. Based on the same mechanism, the CDs also exhibit a good salt tolerance property. Various metal cations have a negligible impact on the fluorescence properties of the CDs (Fig. 2 b). Furthermore, the CDs were added into NaCl solutions with high concentrations of 0.1–1.0 M. The results reveal that the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs remains unchanged (Fig. 2c), indicating that the CDs have an outstanding optical stability in high salt concentration. Similarly, the different anions also demonstrate a negligible effect on the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs. (Fig. 2d). Moreover, the Na2SO4 solutions with high concentrations do not affect the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs (Fig. 2e). In addition, when the CDs solution is observed in a 365 nm UV lamp (10 W) for 1–10 h, it displays only a slight change in the fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. 2f). A fluorescence spectrometer was used to real-time monitor the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at a high voltage of 900 V for 9000 s (Fig. S11). It is found that the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs keeps constant. These above phenomena demonstrate that the CDs have good photobleaching resistance. In summary, the CDs have an outstanding resistance to the strong-acidic condition, strong-basic condition, high-salt concentration and photobleaching.

Owing to the introduction of a fluorescence reference, the ratiometric fluorescence probes usually have a much higher detection accuracy and a more obvious color change, compared to conventional fluorescence probes [26,38,39]. However, common CDs and organic fluorescent materials generally have environmental responsiveness, therefore, it is extremely difficult to choose the fluorescence reference. In contrast, the above highly-stable CDs are the optimal candidate as the fluorescence reference to construct ratiometric fluorescent probes (Fig. 3 a). The construction of ratiometric pH fluorescent probes was taken as an example. We synthesized cellulose acetate fluorescein isothiocyanate (CA-FITC) as the pH-responsive fluorescence indicator (Fig. S12). In the 1H NMR spectrum of CA-FITC, the peaks at 1.6–2.2 ppm are attributed to the methyl protons of acetyl group, the peaks at 2.7–6.0 ppm are assigned to the cellulose skeleton protons, and the peaks at 6.5–6.8 ppm are assigned to the aromatic protons of FITC, indicating the successful chemical bonding of FITC to the cellulose chain (Fig. S13). The FITC content in CA-FITC is too low to be accurately calculated by 1H NMR method. UV absorption method was employed to determine the degree of substitution (DS) of FITC (Fig. S14). The DS of FITC is 0.0327. The fluorescence properties of CDs are stable in different pH buffer solutions (Fig. 2a and S9). In contrast, CA-FITC is a typical monochromatic fluorescent pH-responsive material. As the pH value increases, the fluorescence intensity of CA-FITC sharply increases to a maximum value and then remains constant (Fig. S15).

After mixing pH-stable CDs with pH-responsive CA-FITC in different ratios, ratiometric fluorescent probes that respond to different pH ranges were obtained. As the pH increases from 1.89 to 11.82, the CDs/CA-FITC solution gradually becomes yellow or deep yellow under visible light (Fig. 3b and S16-S18), because a ring-opening reaction of FITC occurs under the basic conditions, resulting in an enhanced absorption in the visible light range (Fig. S20a). Under UV light, the fluorescence color of the CDs/CA-FITC solutions changes from blue to cyan, green and bright yellow-green as the pH increases (Fig. 3b and e, and S16-S18). The fluorescence spectra show that the yellow-green fluorescence emission appears and increases with the increase of pH value (Fig. 3c and d, and S16-S18). The fluorescence intensity at 525–600 nm increases significantly with pH > 7 and remains unchanged with pH > 10. The blue fluorescence emission intensity at 425 nm firstly remains constant and then decreases (Fig. 3c and d, and S16-S18), because CA-FITC quenches the fluorescence emission of CDs via an internal filtration effect (IFE) (Fig. S19) [40,41]. The FITC in the ring-opening state is negatively charged, thus it can form electrostatic attraction interactions with the positively-charged CDs to shorten the distance between them. Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of CA-FITC overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs (Fig. S19b). As the pH value increases, the ratio of yellow-green fluorescence intensity to blue fluorescence intensity (I green/I blue) increases slowly at first, and then displays a sharp increase after pH > 7 (Fig. 3d, and S16-S18). Thus, via adjusting the ratio of CDs to CA-FITC, the pH response interval and the color change can be precisely controlled, which is more obvious than the pH responsive behavior of FITC (Fig. S15).

Taking advantage of the excellent processability and formability of cellulose derivatives (Fig. S20 and Table S3), CDs/CA-FITC ratiometric fluorescent probes can be fabricated into coatings, films and inks, which have a wide range of applications in visual detection, solid-state luminescence, and anti-counterfeiting. By coating CDs/CA-FITC on the filter paper, pH-responsive smart labels were obtained (Fig. 4 a). They exhibit an apparent fluorescence color change at different pH environments, providing a new material for the visual detection of pH value. Via using CDs/CA-FITC and adjusting the pH value of the solutions, various films with different fluorescent colors were prepared (Fig. 4b). In addition, the CDs/CA, CA-FITC and CDs/CA-FITC solutions can be utilized as inks to prepare pH-responsive fluorescent patterns which can be used in the advanced anti-counterfeiting field. For instance, we designed and fabricated the lotus flower pattern by a screen-printing process (Fig. 4c). Once meeting NH3 vapor, the lotus flower pattern gives a color change from cyan to green, and becomes brighter as the treatment time prolongs. Then, if HCl vapor is used to treat the pattern, it recovers to the original state. Therefore, the pH-responsive inks with a fluorescence color change indicate a huge potential in advanced anti-counterfeit labels. Using shrimp as an example, a smart label prepared from ratiometric fluorescent probe was tested whether it could be used to monitor the seafood freshness, because seafood releases alkaline organic amines during preservation. Blue fluorescence represents freshness, cyan fluorescence represents slight spoilage, and green fluorescence represents heavy spoilage. As shown in Fig. 4d, the smart labels were placed inside the packing boxes of shrimp, which were stored at three different temperatures for 1–4 days. After only one day storage at 25 °C and 4 °C, the fluorescent color of the smart labels became green and cyan. After 4 days storage at −20 °C, the fluorescence color of the smart labels became slightly bright cyan. The above phenomena indicate that the ratiometric fluorescent probe can be used as a smart label for the visual detection of seafood freshness.

In this work, we fabricated N-doped CDs with a quantum yield of 23% by using natural cellulose as the precursor and the AmimCl/DMF as the solvent. The heteroatom N on the surface of CDs exists in the form of imidazolium cation, thus leading to an excellent stability of CDs. The fluorescence performance of CDs exhibits a negligible change in various solutions with a wide pH range of 1.89–11.82, different ions and a high concentration of salt. Moreover, the CDs have an outstanding resistance to photobleaching. The CDs were used as a fluorescent reference to construct ratiometric fluorescent probes, for example, CDs/CA-FITC. By adjusting the ratio of CDs to CA-FITC, the visual color and the responsive region of pH value can be regulated. The CDs/CA-FITC fluorescent materials can be used as responsive fluorescent coatings, films and inks for visual detection, solid-state luminescence and anti-counterfeiting.

Kunfeng Jin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xin Ji: Methodology, Data curation. Jinming Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiaocheng Zhang: Methodology. Qinying Nan: Methodology. Jun Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U2004211 and 52173292), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (No. 2018040).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101678.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots: fantastic electroluminescent materials for light-emitting diodes

Mater., 31(26), 2100549.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of multi-color-emissive carbon dots in a single reaction system by hydrothermal
